hook: You already know that shapes can be the same form but different sizes — a tiny triangle and a giant one can look identical except for scale. But how do you *prove* they're truly the same shape, or calculate an unknown side you can never measure directly? This unit gives you the tools to unlock unknown lengths, model real-world objects, and see why so much of geometry, architecture, and art depends on the powerful idea of sameness in shape.
concept: The unifying idea is that shapes can be identical in form but differ only in size — every angle, every ratio of sides stays consistent. This is captured formally by the concept of *similarity*, where figures are related by proportional scaling.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to set up and solve proportions so you have the algebraic foundation needed to compare and scale geometric figures.
Master the definition of similar polygons by identifying equal corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides in any two polygons.
Apply the AA, SSS, and SAS similarity shortcuts to prove two triangles are similar using minimal given information.
You will use the Triangle Proportionality Theorem to find unknown segment lengths created when a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle.
You will connect the transformation of dilation to similarity, seeing how scaling a figure from a center point produces a similar image with a precise scale factor.
Apply similarity ratios to three-dimensional solids to compare corresponding lengths, surface areas, and volumes using the square and cube of the scale factor.
